summary:
India and Pakistan fought four major wars between 1947 and 1999, with the disputed territory of Kashmir at the center of their conflict. The first war (1947–1948) began immediately after the [[event:2671|partition of India]], when Pakistani tribal militias and then regular forces entered the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir, whose Hindu maharaja had not yet acceded to either country. Indian forces repelled the invasion after the maharaja acceded to India, but the conflict ended with a United Nations-supervised ceasefire that left roughly one-third of Kashmir under Pakistani control, creating a Line of Control that has remained contested ever since.

The second war in 1965 was initiated by Pakistan's Operation Gibraltar, which sought to infiltrate forces into Indian-controlled Kashmir to trigger a popular uprising. The operation failed and escalated into conventional warfare. A ceasefire mediated by the Soviet Union at Tashkent ended the conflict without resolving the underlying dispute. The third war in 1971 was the most consequential: it arose from the [[event:2737|Bangladesh Liberation War]], with India intervening militarily on behalf of East Pakistan's independence movement. Pakistani forces surrendered in December 1971, and Bangladesh emerged as an independent state, representing a major strategic defeat for Pakistan.

The fourth conflict, the Kargil War of 1999, occurred after Pakistani forces and militants infiltrated peaks on the Indian side of the Line of Control in Kargil district. Indian forces, after a costly campaign at high altitude, successfully expelled the infiltrators. The war drew international attention partly because both India and Pakistan had by then conducted nuclear tests in 1998, making it the first armed confrontation between two declared nuclear weapons states.

The Kashmir dispute remains one of the world's most dangerous unresolved conflicts, with both countries maintaining large military forces along the Line of Control and periodic clashes continuing into the twenty-first century. Both nations' nuclear arsenals have altered the strategic calculus without resolving the underlying political disagreements.
